title: Ablation Removal of Transport-Blocking Defects in Surface-Electrode Ion Traps
authors: Toby Maddock, Parsa Rahimi, Matthew Aylett, Rares Barcan, Sebastian Weidt, Winfried Karl Hensinger
abstract: We demonstrate in situ removal of a transport-blocking defect on a surface-electrode ion trap device using a Q-switched Nd:YAG 532 nm pulsed ablation laser. This approach eliminates the need to vent and rebake the vacuum system, providing a low-overhead defect-remediation technique well suited for ion-shuttling architectures where system modifications typically incur substantial downtime - particularly in shuttling focussed experiments operating at temperatures that necessitate bakes. Additionally, the hardware used is readily available in many ion trap laboratories, making this solution attractive to experiments operating in such regimes. Following ablation, we observe near-unit shuttling success rates across the previously obstructed region and measure micromotion levels that remain within acceptable limits. This technique enables rapid, reliable restoration of transport pathways without interruption to experimental operation.
summary: This paper demonstrates a method to repair surface-electrode ion traps in situ using pulsed laser ablation to remove physical defects. This technique avoids the need to break vacuum, significantly reducing downtime for complex, cryogenic quantum computing architectures. The process successfully restores ion transport capabilities while maintaining low levels of micromotion.
main_problem: Large particulate defects on surface-electrode ion trap chips block ion transport and induce micromotion, but traditional repair methods like vacuum venting and rebaking cause significant experimental downtime.
main_result: The researchers successfully demonstrated in situ removal of a transport-blocking defect using pulsed laser ablation, restoring near-unit shuttling success rates without damaging surrounding electrodes.
method: A 532 nm nanosecond-pulsed Nd:YAG laser was used for targeted ablation of the defect, with thermal management achieved through short bursts and inter-pulse delays.
model_or_system: A two-module quantum processor experiment utilizing surface-electrode ion traps operated at cryogenic temperatures (47-50 K) in an ultra-high vacuum environment.
key_observables: Ion shuttling success rates, in-plane micromotion levels, RF pseudopotential distortions, and DC voltage perturbations.
important_parameters: Defect dimensions of approximately 65 micrometers; ablation fluence of 5.6 J/cm^2; 532 nm wavelength; 1.5 ns pulse duration.
main_assumptions: Assumed an ideal Gaussian beam profile and a beam perfectly parallel to the trap surface for fluence calculations on neighboring electrodes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a side-view and magnified view of the defect with EMCCD data for height estimation; Figure 5 illustrates micromotion compensation voltages before and after ablation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of transport-blocking defects, describes the experimental setup and laser ablation hardware, details the characterization and ablation process, presents the results regarding shuttling success and micromotion, and discusses the implications for scalable architectures.
why_interesting: This work provides a practical engineering solution for maintaining the reliability and uptime of large-scale, modular ion-trap quantum computers, which is critical for scaling up quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Side-view image of the transport-blocking defect on the ion trap chip. The inset shows a magnified view of the defect region. b) EMCCD pixel data corresponding to a region of interest (ROI) centred on the defect as a guide laser illuminates it, and scanned perpendicular to the chip surface. The measurement is consistent with height estimates based on high resolution images, indicating a defect height of approximately 65 µm.

FIG. 2. The beam path taken by both the guide laser and the ablation beam. The telescope serves to guarantee precise overlap of the abla- tion and guide beam, ensure sufficient beam quality to minimise risk to other areas of the chip, and to expand the beam prior to the final lens

FIG. 3. Schematic diagram of the UHV system housing the surface ion trap, together with the imaging and laser ablation setup. The combined guide and ablation laser beams are delivered through a side window into the UHV chamber. Both beams operate at the same wavelength (532 nm) to enable reliable alignment and to avoid chro- matic aberrations that could lead to systematic misalignment. Beam alignment is performed in the XZ plane using a micrometer trans- lation stage, while focusing is achieved with a lens of focal length F = 150.

FIG. 4. Images of the ion trap chip acquired through the side windows a)-c) and the top windows d)-f) of the vacuum chamber. Panels a) and d) show an overview of the chip region containing the defect, while panels b), c), e), and f) provide magnified views of the defect location and the chip surface after defect removal. Electrodes are numbered sequentially starting from the central gap. The defect is located at the edge of electrode 7. The electrode width is indicated for scale.

FIG. 5. a) Micromotion compensation voltages required to zero photon correlation modulation indices to zero. Yellow denotes the quadratrically expanding voltages required to compensate prior to defect removal - further data points could not be collected due to guaranteed ion loss, as denoted by the blue shaded region. Green de- notes the micromotion voltages required following removal of the defect. b) Sampling further along the trap, highlighting the next maxima of compensation force required to zero photon correlation signals.
